elements.sent-2411-001: Cellular activities are dynamic and intricate, playing a crucial role in advancing diagnostic and therapeutic techniques, yet they often require substantial resources for accurate tracking.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite recent progress, the conventional multi-stage cell tracking approaches not only heavily rely on detection or segmentation results as a prerequisite for the tracking stage, demanding plenty of refined segmentation masks, but are also deteriorated by imbalanced and long sequence data, leading to under-learning in training and missing cells in inference procedures.
elements.sent-2411-003: To alleviate the above issues, this paper proposes the novel end-to-end CAP framework, which leverages the idea of regarding Cell as Point to achieve efficient and stable cell tracking in one stage.
elements.sent-2411-004: CAP abandons detection or segmentation stages and simplifies the process by exploiting the correlation among the trajectories of cell points to track cells jointly, thus reducing the label demand and complexity of the pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-005: With cell point trajectory and visibility to represent cell locations and lineage relationships, CAP leverages the key innovations of adaptive event-guided (AEG) sampling for addressing data imbalance in cell division events and the rolling-as-window (RAW) inference method to ensure continuous tracking of new cells in the long term.
elements.sent-2411-006: Eliminating the need for a prerequisite detection or segmentation stage, CAP demonstrates strong cell tracking performance while also being 10 to 55 times more efficient than existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: The code and models will be released.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=8.5cm]{fig/f1.pdf}} \caption{Our proposed CAP is an end-to-end trained framework leveraging the idea of Cell as Point to facilitate tracking cells efficiently. As (a) illustrates, instead of previous work requiring segmentation (SEG) or detection (DET) as a prerequisite for final tracking (TRA), CAP is able to track all cells within sequence frames in one stage. (b) shows that CAP reduces the inference time ($2.9$s) by approximately \textbf{$\mathbf{10}$ to $\mathbf{55}$ times} compared to previous works while maintaining high tracking performance of $0.93$. (c) demonstrates the cell tracking result predicted by CAP.} \label{vis_merge}
elements.sent-2411-008: Cells are the fundamental units of organs in the human body~\cite{emami2021computerized .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Cell events are diverse, continuously changing, and intricate, involving cell migration (translocation), mitosis (division), and apoptosis (death)~\cite{li2008cell .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Cell tracking is the task of keeping track of each cell's location and reconstructing cell lineages (mother-daughter relations), which help develop preventive therapies~\cite{rodriguez2012cell and drug discovery~\cite{eccles2005cell .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Traditional methods of manual tracking demand significant resources and expertise to achieve reliable results.
elements.sent-2411-012: This complexity creates a pressing need for efficient, automated cell tracking systems.
elements.sent-2411-013: Indeed, several existing works and platforms~\cite{gomez2024celltrackscolab have demonstrated relatively effective performance in achieving automated cell tracking.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The taxonomies of strategies typically involve segmentation followed by linking (SegLnk)~\cite{Akbas_2018_ECCV_Workshops,moen2019accurate , segmentation and linking (Seg\&Lnk)~\cite{chen2021celltrack,lewis2021spatial, embedTrack , or even detection prior to the segmentation and linking stages (DetSegLnk~\cite{pena2020j or DetLnkSeg~\cite{li2008cell,girkin2018light )~\cite{mavska2023cell .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Also, frameworks~\cite{Hayashida2020MPM,Hayashida_2022_WACV often incorporate isolated data-preprocessing stages to represent cell tracking data.}
elements.sent-2411-016: These multi-stage cell tracking pipelines can limit the overall performance of the framework, constrained by the errors that occur at earlier stages and are accumulated and amplified at subsequent stages~\cite{Li_2022_CVPR .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Additionally, a multi-stage framework involving both detection/segmentation and tracking not only requires the tracking ground-truth but also demands additional labels, \ie, segmentation masks, to train the model.
elements.sent-2411-018: It is highly labor-intensive and time-consuming to produce the data annotation, rendering such methods impractical for clinical applications~\cite{8889674 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: To overcome the data challenge for existing multi-stage frameworks, we propose a novel one-stage cell tracking framework, Cell as Point (CAP), as demonstrated in~\cref{vis_merge (a), that treats each cell as a point to jointly track numerous cell points within a sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The one-stage CAP framework not only benefits from the efficient prediction procedure through a straightforward pipeline, shown in~\cref{vis_merge (b), but also from its low requirement for data annotation, solely relying on tracking ground-truth (coarse masks and cell acyclic graphs) to represent the position and status of each cell.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Specifically, by proposing cell point trajectory and visibility to represent the cell tracking data within one-stage framework, CAP offers an elegant solution to alleviate the issues of the complicated data-preprocessing and the dilemma of representation of cell decay, which existing methods struggle to cope with.
elements.sent-2411-022: Another highlight of CAP is the creation of adaptive event-guided (AEG) sampling method and rolling-as-window (RAW) inference approach.
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, AEG sampling is a temporal data sampling strategy for cell microscopy imaging sequence data to remedy the imbalance of cell division occurrences in the dataset; RAW is an inference approach to capture and track new cells within a long sequence in an efficient way.
elements.sent-2411-024: To summarize, our key contributions include:.
elements.sent-2411-025: We propose a novel CAP cell tracking framework that is highly efficient in terms of inference time and data annotation requirements, enabling one-stage tracking that bypasses detection/segmentation to observe and investigate cellular behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-026: We address prevalent challenges in the cell tracking field by proposing AEG sampling to resolve imbalanced cell division occurrence, representing complex cell events for training purposes through cell point trajectory and visibility concepts within a one-stage framework, and employing a RAW inference approach to capture and track new cells in long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our one-stage approach, CAP, achieves comparable or higher performance compared to multi-stage methods while alleviating the need for detection or segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-028: Cell tracking is a crucial task in biological imaging that typically involves the identification and temporal association of cell instances across image frames.
elements.sent-2411-029: Traditionally, this process includes two main steps: cell segmentation (detection) and cell linking~\cite{CHEN2021101 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The segmentation step identifies individual cells in each frame, while the linking step establishes temporal associations between these cells across frames, sometimes capturing complex dynamics such as cell division and migration~\cite{embedTrack,mavska2023cell .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Nowadays, instead of traditional approaches such as Nearest Neighbors linking~\cite{stegmaier2017fuzzy , Bayesian filters~\cite{smal2006bayesian,chang2017automated,hirose2017spf,xu2019spatial,hossain2018visual,ulicna2021automated , Viterbi~\cite{6957576,magnusson2015tracking , and graph-based matching~\cite{arbelle2018probabilistic,turetken2016network,akram2017cell,loffler2021graph , the advent of deep learning has transformed the landscape of cell tracking, leveraging advanced neural architectures for segmentation and tracking tasks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Deep learning approaches often utilize convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to perform these tasks~\cite{soelistyo2023machine , employing complex models that combine multiple stages like detection~\cite{8078516,5872571,bise2013cell,liu2017mitosis,zhou2019joint,hayashida2019cell,nishimura2020weakly,weigert2020star,Hayashida2020MPM,Hayashida_2022_WACV , segmentation~\cite{yang2005cell,padfield2011coupled,7164093,7493415,embedTrack,stringer2021cellpose,bao2021dmnet,ben2022graph,scherr2020cell , and tracking into the frameworks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Several contemporary methods~\cite{rempfler2017cell,payer2018instance,hayashida2019cell aim to integrate contextual cell data into tracking models.}
elements.sent-2411-034: ConvGRU~\cite{payer2018instance is introduced by Payer \etal, capturing local cellular and inter-frame features.}
elements.sent-2411-035: However, this method requires extensive annotated training data for all cell regions, making it labor-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-036: Motion and Position Map (MPM) is capable of representing detection and association at once, which is proposed by Hayashida \etal \cite{Hayashida2020MPM , marking an advancement in simplifying the pipeline for cell tracking.}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, it still requires multiple stages, first generating the MPM followed by subsequent detection and tracking procedures.
elements.sent-2411-038: Thus, although existing multi-stage methods, which are based on individual cell detection followed by linking/association or evolving segmentation masks over time, show great cell tracking performance, they heavily rely on segmentation as a preliminary step before tracking.
elements.sent-2411-039: These methods often require high-quality data annotation for training and can be computationally intensive, especially when dealing with high-resolution~\cite{yang2023deep .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, these methods often involve complex pipelines that may not support end-to-end or one-stage training, posing challenges for both training and inference phases in terms of efficiency and scalability~\cite{Li_2022_CVPR,8889674 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Thus, to tackle these issues, an end-to-end one-stage framework that omits the intermediate segmentation or detection stage is essential.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the field of natural images, recent end-to-end video point-tracking frameworks have achieved commendable performance.
elements.sent-2411-043: Notably, TAP-Vid~\cite{doersch2022tap pioneered tracking any point across video frames, establishing a benchmark, and claiming a baseline method.}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, this method does not support tracking occluded points, limiting its applicability in cell-tracking scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-045: Even though PIPs~\cite{harley2022particle and PointOdyssey~\cite{zheng2023point methods take measures to address the issue of point occlusion in long sequences by tracking selected points using a fixed sliding window and resuming tracking from the last visible frame, these methods struggle with point tracking independently, which may not capture the dependencies between multiple points.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: OmniMotion~\cite{wang2023omnimotion accounts for estimated correspondences within a canonical space by optimizing a volumetric representation for video sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Although innovative, this method involves test-time optimization, making it computationally expensive, which is less viable for clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-048: CoTracker~\cite{karaev2023cotracker represents significant advancements in the field of point tracking.}
elements.sent-2411-049: It addresses the inaccuracies led by independent tracking among points by incorporating incremental point coordinates updates based on 4D correlation volumes, first proposed by RAFT~\cite{teed2020raft constructed for all pairs of points from point/object features.}
elements.sent-2411-050: CoTracker3~\cite{karaev2024cotracker3 is advanced by involving a semi-supervised training methodology to reduce reliance on synthetic datasets and utilizing pseudo-labeling techniques to generate training labels from unannotated real-world videos.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Although the performance of cell tracking may benefit from investigating the interdependencies between each cell, inspired by joint tracking~\cite{karaev2023cotracker , the unique properties of cell behaviors, \ie, cell mitosis and apoptosis, have not been considered in the model construction.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, cells are not only relative with position and occlusion but also potentially associated with their ancestors due to the regulation of cellular life decay and division, triggering another issue: missing tracking for new daughter cells.
elements.sent-2411-053: Moreover, regarding the publicly available cell tracking data~\cite{moen2019accurate,mavska2023cell,ulman2017objective,ker2018phase , the diversity of cell types results in significant variations in cell division rates and sequence length across different datasets categorized by specimens.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Imbalanced data~\cite{krawczyk2016learning can lead to the omission of complete division events (from the death of the mother cell to the birth of the daughter cell) during random sampling with a low division rate; long sequences typically require more time or computational resources to predict cell locations and lineages at once.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Thus, existing methods potentially result in the under-learning of division cases in the model training process and a higher resource requirement in the inference procedure.
elements.sent-2411-056: Therefore, the uniqueness of this time-lapse cell sequential image data poses the most significant challenge in developing a one-stage cell tracking framework to effectively track cells jointly.
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{fig/cell_represent.pdf}} \caption{Cell Point Trajectory and Visibility. M, D1, and D2 denote mother cell, daughter cell 1, daughter cell 2. Three valid trajectory and visibility combinations: (a) M has not divided; (b) M has divided, and only D1 occurs in the $t$-th frame; (c) M has divided, and both D1 and D2 occur in the $t$-th frame.} \label{fig:cell_represent}
elements.sent-2411-057: Cell tracking aims to identify the characteristics of cell migration and division, thereby fully automating the prediction of specific cell position and division status changes in each frame of a time-lapse microscopy video.
elements.sent-2411-058: In our framework, CAP, every single cell is a point, tracking all cell points throughout the duration of a frame sequence $S = (I_t)_{t=1 ^T$ containing $T$ grayscale cell microscopy images $I_t \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: CAP primarily utilizes and learns the dependencies between cell point trajectories and visibilities to predict cell locations and lineages.
elements.sent-2411-060: The cell point trajectory is constituted by the $N$ cell centroid point locations $L^i_t = (x^i_t, y^i_t) \in \mathbb{R ^{2}$, $i=1,\dots,N$, $t =1,\dots,T$, where $t$ is the time of a frame sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The cell point visibility of the cell $V^i_t \in \{0,1\}$ indicates whether the $i$-th cell point appears at the location $L^i_t$ in the frame at time $t$; in other words, whether the cell has divided into new cells and disappeared.
elements.sent-2411-062: This simple yet effective representation method will efficiently aid the CAP framework in achieving one-stage efficient cell tracking.
elements.sent-2411-063: To facilitate the effective training of models to understand the biological behavior of cell division while mitigating data imbalance~\cite{yudistira2020prediction , we propose the Adaptive Event-Guided (AEG) sampling technique to balance the occurrences of cell division in a sequence of frames the model sees.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In order to reduce operational costs and compromise overfitting~\cite{babaeizadeh2021fitvid , instead of seeing the entire training sequence in a single instance, the model learns a continuous sequence of $T_{s}$ frames, where $0 < T_{s < T$, with the initial frame index determined using AEG for each iteration.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The AEG method leverages the intrinsic dynamics of cell division within the frame sequences to guide the sampling process, enhancing the representational diversity of the training dataset and ensuring the model’s robustness to variations in cell behavior.
elements.sent-2411-066: Technically, as data loading iterates through the whole dataset by detecting the complete cell division processes (from the death of parent cell to the birth of daughter cell), an anchor is added at each time cell division occurs $\mathbb{T _{anchors}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: When the probability $P_{AEG}$ indicates the application of the AEG method, our CAP framework enables AEG sampling applied to selecting $T_{s}$ as illustrated in ~\cref{fig:aeg ; Otherwise, the starting frame $t_{starting}$ of $T_{s}$ is randomly located in the range of $(0,T-T_s)$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: For each training sequence, we crop a fixed-length frame sequence in accordance with the starting frame from the video.
elements.sent-2411-069: Occasionally, the process of cell division, resulting in the formation of new cells, may span multiple frames.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus, the leverage of the AEG approach allows us to capture complete division processes within sampled sequences in data pre-processing.
elements.sent-2411-071: This fixed-length cropping is critical to maintaining uniformity in the input data, facilitating more stable and reliable learning outcomes.
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{fig/AEG.pdf}} \caption{AEG Sampling. We propose integrating AEG sampling on the sequence of frames to enable the model to learn a complete and effective cell division process.} \label{fig:aeg}
elements.sent-2411-072: By regarding each cell as a point and leveraging the two-dimensional feature across time and a set of tracked cell points, we implement an effective and efficient method for cell tracking via the correlation between cell point trajectories and visibilities.
elements.sent-2411-073: Specifically, the training process of CAP as a transformer $\Theta$ neural network~\cite{NIPS2017_3f5ee243 , where $\Theta: G \mapsto O$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: In order to improve the estimated cell trajectories and visibilities along with $M$ iterations, the trajectories and visibilities of $i$ points (cells) in a $t$-frame sequence are encoded into a grid of input tokens $G^i_t$.
elements.sent-2411-075: The following sections elaborate on the transformer formulation visualized in~\cref{fig:main .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/main.pdf}} \caption{Cell Joint Tracking Structure. For a sequence $T_s$, evolving from initialized to estimated cell point trajectories and visibilities requires $M$ updates on cell point trajectory $\hat L$ and tracking feature $F$ with cell point visibility $\hat V$ computed at the last ($M$-th) time update. } \label{fig:main}
elements.sent-2411-076: To initialize the tracking feature $F$ for the part of input tokens $G$, we first generate image features (feature map) for each frame in the training sequence, from which the tracking features are distilled according to cell point locations.
elements.sent-2411-077: Specifically, the feature extractor is a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained end-to-end in the framework for computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-078: Leveraging the CNN feature extractor, the feature maps with $dim$ dimensions are generated from $I_t$, $\phi(I_t): \mathbb{R ^{3 \times H \times W \mapsto \mathbb{R ^{dim \times \left(\left\lfloor \frac{H {k \right\rfloor \times \left\lfloor \frac{W {k \right\rfloor\right)}$, where $k=4$ for computational efficiency, and the features are downscaled by average pooling with strides $s$ for multi-scaled features.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The tracking features $F^i_{t=1}$ for $i$ are captured by bilinear sampling from the location of cell points on the initial frame $L_{t=1}$ on the feature map $\phi(I_{t=1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: By broadcasting the tracking features in $t=1$ frame for all training frames $T_s$ into shape $(T_s, N, dim)$, the tracking feature vector $F^i_t \in \mathbb{R ^{dim}$ is initialized for the transformer input, which is refined and updated with the training process.}
elements.sent-2411-081: To achieve the objective of associating cell point trajectories with a sequence of frames, CAP employs correlation volume $C^i_t \in \mathbb{R ^S$, where $S=4$, proposed in RAFT~\cite{teed2020raft .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: These correlation volumes $C^i_t$ are derived by assessing the relationship between the tracking features $F^i_t$ and the image features $\phi(I_t)$ in proximity to the estimated cell point location $\hat{L ^i_t$ of the trajectory on the $t$-th frame.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Technically, each $C^i_t$ is the average over the correlation response in a $2^s\times2^s$ grid: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \relax [C_t^i]_\delta^s = \frac{1}{2^{2s}} \sum_\delta^{2^s} \sum_\delta^{2^s} \langle F^i_t, \phi_s(I_t)_{2^s\hat{x}+\delta,2^s\hat{y}+\delta} \rangle = \langle F^i_t, \frac{1}{2^{2s}} (\sum_\delta^{2^s} \sum_\delta^{2^s} \phi_s(I_t)_{2^s\hat{x}+\delta,2^s\hat{y}+\delta}) \rangle, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $s = 1,\dots, S$ are the feature scales and $\delta \in \mathbb{Z ^2$, \( \| \delta \|_{\infty \leq \Delta \) are offsets around cell points, $\hat x$ and $\hat y$ are the estimated location of the cell point $\hat L_t^i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This means that the value at $[C_t^i]_\delta^s$ can be computed as the inner product between the feature vector $F^i_t$ and $\phi_s(I_t)$ at $\hat L_t^i$ pooled with kernel size $2^s \times 2^s$.
elements.sent-2411-085: All different scaled features are sampled by using bilinear interpolation at non-integer locations and border padding from the image feature map $\phi_s(I_t)$.
elements.sent-2411-086: The input tokens express the concatenation of features: location $\hat L^i_t - \hat L^i_1$, visibility $\hat V^i_t$, tracking features $F^i_t$, and correlation volume of cell trajectories $C^i_t$.
elements.sent-2411-087: As for the output tokens $O(\Delta \hat L, \Delta F)$, contain updates for location and refined tracking features: \begin{equation}O^{i}_t = (\Delta \hat L^{3i}_t, \Delta F^{i}_t),\end{equation} where CAP estimates $3$ locations for each cell, following the structure in~\cref{fig:cell_represent : the cell's own location and the locations of two potential daughter cells.}
elements.sent-2411-088: This is because any cell division that happened in the event would have been that of the parent cell into two daughter cells~\cite{inoue1981cell .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Thus, CAP represents $L$ and $V$ through a $3$-column format to reserve space for each cell's progeny.
elements.sent-2411-090: Utilizing the approach of applying the transformer $\Theta$ iteratively for a sequence $T_s$, the update operator in CAP produces and progressively improves the cell locations estimation $\hat L^{3i _t$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Considering $M$ times estimating the cell locations, the update of the output computes $O(\Delta \hat L, \Delta F)$, which is applied to the next iteration: \begin{equation}\hat L^{(m+1)} = \hat L^{(m)} + \Delta \hat L ~and ~ F^{(m+1)} = F^{(m)} + \Delta F,\end{equation} where $m=1, \dots, M$ and $m=1$ denotes initialization.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\hat L^{(m+1)} = \hat L^{(m)} + \Delta \hat L ~and ~ F^{(m+1)} = F^{(m)} + \Delta F,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-092: At the last iteration of the update $M$, cell point visibility $V^{3i _t$ for locations estimated $\hat L^{3i _t$ is computed by $\hat V^{(M) = \sigma(W F^{(M) )$, where $\sigma$ represents the sigmoid activation function and $W$ is a learned matrix of weights.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: By investigating the $3$ visibilities $V^{3i _t$, CAP tells if the cell division occurred on cell $i$ in the $t$-th frame.}
elements.sent-2411-094: We supervised our transformer $\Theta$ on the trajectory and visibility between predicted and ground truth for every semi-overlapping window.
elements.sent-2411-095: The trajectory loss function $\mathcal{L _{tra}$ is location regression summed over the update iterations, and the visibility loss function $\mathcal{L _{vis}$ is the cross entropy of binary visibility options: \begin{equation}L_{tra}(\hat{L}, L) =\sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\gamma^{M-m}\|\hat L^{(m,j)} - L^{(j)}\|, L_{vis}(\hat{V}, V)=\sum_{j=1}^{J}\operatorname{CE}(\hat V^{(M,j)},V^{(j)}),\end{equation} where $M$ is the number of times iteratively estimating the cell locations with updated $\mathcal{L _{tra}$ for a sequence, and $\mathcal{L _{vis}$ is updated at the $M$-th iteration; $J$ is the total number of $T_s$ whose trajectory starts in the middle of the previous $T_s$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}L_{tra}(\hat{L}, L) =\sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\gamma^{M-m}\|\hat L^{(m,j)} - L^{(j)}\|, L_{vis}(\hat{V}, V)=\sum_{j=1}^{J}\operatorname{CE}(\hat V^{(M,j)},V^{(j)}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: In the field of cell tracking, the inference part plays a crucial role in generating the final tracking mask and in documenting cell lineage relationships based on predicted cell locations and division events.
elements.sent-2411-097: In existing works~\cite{stringer2021cellpose,scherr2020cell,weigert2020star,embedTrack,ben2022graph , the inference and post-processing stages are mostly explicitly tailored to different datasets, which vary in sequence lengths, which may hinder broad application to other datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-098: In contrast, our proposed inference approach, rolling-as-window (RAW) in the CAP, not only simplifies and unifies these processes but also proves effective across varying lengths of frame sequences, facilitating high-quality inference regardless of computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-099: Within our model, the mechanism of iterative updates assists in maintaining the quality of inference results.
elements.sent-2411-100: It ensures that the output remains consistent and high-quality while utilizing a rolling loop that crops the yet-to-be-inferred clip from the whole sequence into a window for inference.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Algorithm of RAW Inference} \label{raw_algo1} \SetAlgoLined Input: $\Theta$, $S_\text{infer}\gets (I_t)^{T_\text{infer}}_{t=1}$, $Q\gets (x^i, y^i)$, $l_\text{win}$ Output: $L_t$, $V_t$, where $t\gets1,\dots, T_\text{infer}$ Initialization: current process frame $t_\text{cur} \gets 1$ \While{$t_\text{cur}<T_\text{infer}$}{ Obtain current window frames $S_\text{w} \gets (I_t)^{t_\text{cur}+l_\text{win}}_{t=t_\text{cur}}$ $L_\text{w}$, $V_\text{w} \gets \Theta(S_\text{w})$ \For{$i \gets 0$ to $l_\text{win}$}{ $t_\text{cur} \gets t_\text{cur}+i$ $Q \gets L_{t_\text{cur}}$ \If{find new cell(s)} { Concatenate $Q$ and $\hat L_{t_\text{cur}}^\text{new}$ break } } }
elements.sent-2411-101: In a certain sequence with $T_\text{infer}$ frames, if the inference window length $l_\text{win}$ supported by either computational resources or architecture is shorter than $T_\text{infer}$, the first rolling of window frames $S_\text{w =(I_t)^{l_\text{win _{t=1}$ is passed into the trained transformer $\Theta$; otherwise, the whole sequence will be inferred at once.}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The RAW inference approach is summarized in Alg.~\ref{raw_algo1 , which will be triggered while the former scenario happens.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Specifically, a $l_\text{win}$-length subsequence starting from $t=1$ is passed into $\Theta$ for prediction.
elements.sent-2411-104: The output, cell point trajectories $L_t$ and visibilities $V_t$, where $t=1, \dots, l_\text{win}$, are processed frame-by-frame to update query points $Q$ based on the $L_t$ of identified cells and check for cell division or the emergence of new cells.
elements.sent-2411-105: Whenever a new cell appears, the predicted location of this new cell $\hat L_t^\text{new}$ is incorporated into the updated query points $Q$ for the subsequent rolling of analysis by the formula: \begin{equation}Q_r+1 = concat(Q_r, \hat L_t^new) =  Q_r \hat L_t^new ,\end{equation} where $r$ is the times of rolling for window inference.
elements.sent-2411-106: Subsequently, RAW segments a new window sequence starting from the frame where the new cell appeared, preparing it for the next application of $\Theta$.
elements.sent-2411-107: By adjusting the $l_\text{win}$ based on the capacity of computational resources and $T_\text{infer}$ value, our CAP is capable of performing efficient and high-quality inference on sequences of any length.

elements.sent-2411-148: In this paper, we introduce the one-stage CAP (Cell as Point) framework, which eliminates the need for a separate segmentation or detection stage—a significant innovation in cell tracking.
elements.sent-2411-149: This approach substantially improves efficiency by reducing inference time by a factor of $10$ to $55$, while also minimizing the need for high-quality data annotation.
elements.sent-2411-150: CAP effectively captures complex cell states and tracks cells in a single step, all while maintaining high efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-151: Additionally, it addresses common challenges in cell tracking, such as data imbalance and long sequence inference.
elements.sent-2411-152: In conclusion, CAP not only offers considerable practical value in real-world applications due to its efficiency but also pioneers a novel approach in the field of cell tracking.

elements.sent-2411-154: Consequently, a strategy to train a versatile model via our one-stage framework that could mitigate the gap and generalize to all datasets deserves future exploration.
elements.sent-2411-156: Optical flow refers to the pattern of apparent motion of objects, surfaces, or edges in a visual scene caused by the relative movement between an observer and the scene.
elements.sent-2411-157: In the natural imaging field, the optical flow algorithms, methods for estimating per-pixel motion in images~\cite{ZHAI2021107861 , were studied through the lens of the color constancy equation~\cite{article_ijcv,Janai_2018_ECCV .}}
elements.sent-2411-158: However, with the advancement in the deep learning field, more modern approaches~\cite{sun2018pwc , starting from FlowNet~\cite{dosovitskiy2015flownet,ilg2017flownet and DCFlow~\cite{xu2017accurate , introduced a 4D cost volume computation method and have shifted towards leveraging deep learning for high-accuracy optical flow estimation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: RAFT~\cite{teed2020raft is introduced by Teed and Deng, which further refined the DCFlow approach by incorporating incremental flow updates based on 4D correlation volumes constructed for computing the similarity between all pairs of pixels across two images~\cite{harley2022particle , enabling the model to handle large displacements and complex motion patterns effectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: The model iteratively updates the flow field using a recurrent unit that performs lookups on the correlation volumes, leading to precise motion estimation~\cite{seidel2021omniflow .}
elements.sent-2411-161: Given two feature maps $F_1 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times dim}$ and $F_2 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times dim}$ with output $dim$-dimensions, which are features extracted from two input images $I_1$ and $I_2$, by feature extractor $g_\theta$: \begin{equation}F_1=g_\theta(I_1) \text{ and } F_2=g_\theta(I_2).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: RAFT computes a 4D correlation volume $C$ that captures the similarity between all pairs of pixels across the two images.
elements.sent-2411-163: The correlation between a pixel at position $(i,j)$ in $F_1$ and a pixel at position $(k,l)$ in $F_2$ is calculated as the dot product of their feature vectors: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \relax C_{ijkl} = \langle F_1(i,j), F_2(k,l) \rangle =\sum_h F_1(i,j,h) \cdot F_2(k,l,h). \end{aligned}\end{equation} The correlation volume is a 4D tensor of dimensions $H \times W \times H \times W$, where $H$ and $W$ are respectively the height and width of feature maps $F$s.
elements.sent-2411-164: RAFT constructs a correlation pyramid by applying average pooling to the last two dimensions of the 4D correlation volume to capture multi-scale feature information.
elements.sent-2411-165: Specifically, this pooling is performed with kernel sizes of $1$ $2$, $4$, and $8$, and a set of multi-scale correlation volumes $C^k = \{ C^1, C^2, C^3, C^4 \}$ is generated.
elements.sent-2411-166: Thus, each volume $C^k$ in the pyramid has dimensions $H \times W \times \frac{H {2^k \times \frac{W {2^k}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: The update operator estimates a sequence of flow estimations $\{ f_1, \dots, f_n \}$ with initializing the start point $f_0=0$.
elements.sent-2411-168: For each iteration, it produces $\Delta f$ as the direction that should be applied to the current estimation: \begin{equation}f_{k+1}=\Delta f + f_{k+1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-169: Specifically, given the current flow estimation $f_k$, the correlation volume pyramid is retrieved and undergoes processing through two convolutional layers to extract relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-170: The updated information is comprised of correlation, flow, and context features and is updated over time as a feature map concatenation.

elements.sent-2411-222: Conventional multi-stage cell tracking approaches rely heavily on detection or segmentation in each frame as a prerequisite, requiring substantial resources for high-quality segmentation masks and increasing the overall prediction time.
elements.sent-2411-223: To address these limitations, we propose CAP, a novel end-to-end one-stage framework that reimagines cell tracking by treating Cell as Point.
elements.sent-2411-224: Unlike traditional methods, CAP eliminates the need for explicit detection or segmentation, instead jointly tracking cells for sequences in one stage by leveraging the inherent correlations among their trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-225: This simplification reduces both labeling requirements and pipeline complexity.
elements.sent-2411-226: However, directly processing the entire sequence in one stage poses challenges related to data imbalance in capturing cell division events and long sequence inference.
elements.sent-2411-227: To solve these challenges, CAP introduces two key innovations: (1) adaptive event-guided (AEG) sampling, which prioritizes cell division events to mitigate the occurrence imbalance of cell events, and (2) the rolling-as-window (RAW) inference strategy, which ensures continuous and stable tracking of newly emerging cells over extended sequences.
elements.sent-2411-228: By removing the dependency on segmentation-based preprocessing while addressing the challenges of imbalanced occurrence of cell events and long-sequence tracking, CAP demonstrates promising cell tracking performance and is 10 to 55 times more efficient than existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-229: The code and model checkpoints will be available soon.
elements.sent-2411-230: Indeed, several existing works and platforms~\cite{gomez2024celltrackscolab,fedorchuk2025deepkymotracker,gallusser2024trackastra have demonstrated relatively effective performance in achieving automated cell tracking.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Additionally, a multi-stage framework involving both detection/segmentation and tracking not only requires the tracking ground-truth but also demands additional labels, \ie, high-quality segmentation masks, to train the model.
elements.sent-2411-232: Specifically, by proposing cell point trajectory and visibility to represent the cell tracking data within a one-stage framework comprehensively, CAP offers an elegant solution to alleviate the issues of the complicated data-preprocessing and the dilemma of representation of cell decay.
elements.sent-2411-233: Another key innovation of CAP is the introduction of adaptive event-guided (AEG) sampling method and rolling-as-window (RAW) inference approach.
elements.sent-2411-234: We propose a novel CAP cell tracking framework that is highly efficient in terms of inference time and data annotation requirements, enabling one-stage tracking that bypasses detection/segmentation to investigate cellular behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-235: We address prevalent challenges in the cell sequence data by proposing AEG sampling to resolve imbalanced cell division occurrence, representing complex cell events for training purposes through cell point trajectory and visibility concepts within a one-stage framework, and employing a RAW inference approach to capture and track new cells in long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-236: Our one-stage approach, CAP, achieves promising performance compared to existing multi-stage methods while alleviating the need for detection or segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-237: Traditionally, this process includes two main steps: cell segmentation (detection)~\cite{abousamra2021multi,wolf2023unsupervised,na2024segment,wang2022cris and cell linking~\cite{CHEN2021101 .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: Deep learning approaches often employ complex models, \eg, CNN, RNN, GNN, and transformer, to combine multiple stages like detection~\cite{8078516,5872571,bise2013cell,liu2017mitosis,zhou2019joint,hayashida2019cell,nishimura2020weakly,weigert2020star,Hayashida2020MPM,Hayashida_2022_WACV,Kamiya_2024_ACCV , segmentation~\cite{yang2005cell,padfield2011coupled,7164093,7493415,embedTrack,stringer2021cellpose,bao2021dmnet,ben2022graph,scherr2020cell,o2024cell,CMTT,bragantini2024ultrack,PRXLife,10.1007/978-3-031-72986-7_3,teranikar2024automated , and tracking into the frameworks.}}

elements.sent-2411-240: However, the trackers designed for general-purpose applications are ineffective for cell tracking because the unique properties of cell behaviors, \ie, cell mitosis and apoptosis, have not been considered in the model construction.
elements.sent-2411-241: Specifically, cells are not only relative with position and occlusion but also potentially associated with their ancestors due to the regulation of cellular life decay and division.

elements.sent-2411-252: To facilitate the effective training of models to understand the biological behavior of the completed cell division event while mitigating data imbalance~\cite{yudistira2020prediction,liu2021overfitting , we propose the Adaptive Event-Guided (AEG) sampling technique to balance the occurrences of cell division in a sequence of frames the model sees by determining the initial frame of $T_{s}$ learning a complete event.}

elements.sent-2411-260: The tracking features $F^i_{t=1}$ for $i$ are captured by bilinear interpolation from the location of cell points on the initial frame $L_{t=1}$ on the feature map $\phi(I_{t=1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-261: The tracking feature vector $F^i_t \in \mathbb{R ^{dim}$ is initialized for the transformer input by broadcasting the tracking features in $t=1$ frame for all $T_s$ frames, which is refined and updated with $M$ iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-262: To achieve the objective of associating cell point trajectories jointly, CAP employs correlation volume $C^i_t \in \mathbb{R ^S$, where $S=4$, proposed in RAFT~\cite{teed2020raft .}}
elements.sent-2411-263: Technically, each $C^i_t$ is the average over the correlation response in a $2^s\times2^s$ grid: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \relax [C_t^i]_\delta^s = \frac{1}{2^{2s}} \sum_\delta^{2^s} \sum_\delta^{2^s} \langle F^i_t, \phi_s(I_t)_{2^s\hat{x}+\delta,2^s\hat{y}+\delta} \rangle = \langle F^i_t, \frac{1}{2^{2s}} (\sum_\delta^{2^s} \sum_\delta^{2^s} \phi_s(I_t)_{2^s\hat{x}+\delta,2^s\hat{y}+\delta}) \rangle, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $s = 1,\dots, S$ are the feature scales, and $\delta$ is the offset around cell points, $(\hat x, \hat y)$ is the estimated location coordinate of the cell point $\hat L_t^i$.

elements.sent-2411-272: In cell tracking, the inference stage is crucial for producing the final tracking mask and establishing cell lineage relationships by analyzing predicted cell locations and division events.
elements.sent-2411-273: Existing works~\cite{stringer2021cellpose,scherr2020cell,weigert2020star,embedTrack,ben2022graph often customize inference and post-processing stages with different configurations for each dataset, adapting to variations in sequence length.}
elements.sent-2411-274: This data-specific tailoring limits their generalizability and broader applicability to other datasets.
elements.sent-2411-275: In contrast, our proposed Rolling-As-Window (RAW) inference approach in CAP simplifies and unifies these processes while maintaining effectiveness across varying sequence lengths, enabling high-quality inference regardless of computational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-276: Specifically, a consistent and high-quality inference result is achieved through a rolling loop, which sequentially extracts yet-to-be-inferred clips from the full sequence, processing them within a defined window for inference.
elements.sent-2411-277: We conducted experiments to demonstrate the high efficacy and efficiency of CAP, our end-to-end, one-stage framework for cell tracking.
elements.sent-2411-278: Despite requiring minimal inference time and eliminating the need for additional segmentation masks, CAP achieves promising performance.
elements.sent-2411-279: Additionally, we perform comprehensive ablation studies to assess the effectiveness of individual CAP components.

elements.sent-2411-296: It shows the performance comparison of our proposed CAP framework against KIT-GE across four datasets, HeLa, PC-3, 3T3, and RAW264, in the DeepCell.
elements.sent-2411-297: CAP consistently achieves higher TRA (tracking accuracy) scores than KIT-GE in HeLa, PC-3, and 3T3 datasets, demonstrating its superior tracking capability.
elements.sent-2411-298: Furthermore, CAP significantly reduces IT (inference time) across all datasets, being $10 \times$ to $88 \times$ faster than KIT-GE, highlighting its efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-299: Overall, CAP delivers superior tracking accuracy with significantly lower inference time, reinforcing its effectiveness as a one-stage tracking solution compared to the multi-stage KIT-GE framework.
elements.sent-2411-300: By removing the dependency on segmentation-based preprocessing while addressing the challenges of imbalanced occurrence of cell events and long-sequence tracking, CAP demonstrates promising cell tracking performance and is 8 to 32 times more efficient than existing methods.
elements.figure-2411-027: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/intro_sub.pdf}} \caption{Our proposed CAP is an end-to-end trained framework leveraging the idea of Cell as Point to facilitate tracking cells efficiently. (b) shows that CAP reduces the inference time ($2.9$s) by approximately \textbf{$\mathbf{8}$ to $\mathbf{32}$ times} compared to previous works while maintaining high tracking performance of $0.93$. elements.sent-2411-303: Recurrent All-Pairs Field Transforms (RAFT)~\cite{teed2020raft embedded in CoTracker~\cite{karaev2023cotracker can be conceptualized as incrementally computing the similarity between all pairs of pixel points across frames in the sequence~\cite{harley2022particle , enabling the model to handle large displacements and complex motion patterns~\cite{seidel2021omniflow .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-304: In the field of cell tracking, this design promotes the cell point's trajectories in a frame sequence constructed by iteratively updating the flow field using a recurrent unit that performs lookups on a 4D correlation volume.
elements.sent-2411-305: Given two feature maps $F_1 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times dim}$ and $F_2 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times dim}$ with output $dim$-dimensions, which are features extracted from two input images $I_1$ and $I_2$, by the feature extractor $g_\phi$: $F_1=g_\phi(I_1)$ and $F_2=g_\phi(I_2)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-306: RAFT computes a correlation volume $C$ that captures the similarity between all pairs of pixels across the two images.
elements.sent-2411-307: The correlation between a pixel at position $(i,j)$ in $F_1$ and a pixel at position $(k,l)$ in $F_2$ is calculated as the dot product of their feature vectors: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} C_{ijkl} = \langle F_1(i,j), F_2(k,l) \rangle =\sum_h F_1(i,j,h) \cdot F_2(k,l,h), \end{aligned}\end{equation} which is a 4D tensor of dimensions $H \times W \times H \times W$, where $H$ and $W$ are respectively the height and width of feature maps $F$s.
elements.sent-2411-308: To capture multi-scale feature information for large displacement, RAFT constructs a correlation pyramid by applying average pooling to the last two dimensions of the 4D correlation volume.
elements.sent-2411-309: Specifically, this pooling is performed with kernel sizes $k$ of $1$ $2$, $4$, and $8$, and a set of multi-scale correlation volumes $C^k = \{ C^1, C^2, C^3, C^4 \}$ is generated.
elements.sent-2411-310: For each iteration, it produces $\Delta f$ as the direction that should be applied to the current estimation: $f_{k+1 =\Delta f + f_{k+1}$.}

elements.sent-2411-313: DeepCell database consists of HeLa, PC-3, 3T3, and RAW264 datasets, which respectively contains cell types HeLa-S3, PC-3, NIH-3T3, and RAW 264.7, visualized in~\cref{deepcell .}
elements.sent-2411-314: The cells are cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (RAW 264.7 and NIH-3T3) or F-12K medium (Hela-S3 and PC-3) supplemented with the necessary drugs, e.g., L-glutamine, penicillin, for all other cells.
elements.sent-2411-315: DeepCell contains $96$ sequences in total: $45$ for HeLa, $7$ for PC-3, $10$ for 3T3, and $34$ for RAW264.
elements.sent-2411-316: Among them, there are $5$, $2$, $1$, and $3$ sequences for datasets HeLa, PC-3, 3T3, and RAW264 for evaluating the training outcomes of the remaining sequences, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-317: Each sequence is segmented into $42$ frames, lasting $6$ minutes, containing cell behaviors, i.e., cell migration and division.
elements.sent-2411-318: In terms of data annotation requirements, unlike previous works that rely on all available TRA GT masks, SEG GT, and ST (when available), CAP requires only the TRA GT masks, thereby substantially reducing the annotation demand.

elements.sent-2411-333: Evaluation is based on both cell-tracking accuracy-related metrics ($\mathrm{TRA}$ derived from $\mathrm{EdgeSplit}$, $\mathrm{EdgeAdd}$, $\mathrm{EdgeDelete}$, $\mathrm{EdgeSemantic}$, $\mathrm{FP}$, and $\mathrm{FN}$) and inference efficiency ($\mathrm{IT}$).

elements.sent-2411-335: On HeLa and PC-3, CAP achieves the highest $\mathrm{TRA}$ scores of $0.926$ and $0.952$, surpassing prior works while requiring only $1.3$s and $1.1$s inference time, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-336: This highlights CAP’s ability to deliver state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-337: On RAW264, CAP attains a competitive $\mathrm{TRA}$ of $0.864$ with just $2.8$s inference, demonstrating a $3-20\times$ reduction in runtime compared to other methods.
elements.sent-2411-338: Even on the challenging 3T3 dataset, CAP achieves a strong $\mathrm{TRA}$ of $0.854$, comparable to the best baseline ($0.857$), but with an inference time of only $2.2$s versus up to $65.0$s for competing methods.
elements.sent-2411-339: As for the ISBI CTC datasets, as shown in Table~\ref{comp_isbi , on U373, CAP achieves the best overall performance, recording zero errors across tracking structural metrics and achieving a $\mathrm{TRA}$ of $0.985$, with the lowest inference time of $2.6$s.}
elements.sent-2411-340: For GOWT1, CAP achieves a $\mathrm{TRA}$ of $0.960$, slightly below KIT-GE ($0.966$), while reducing inference time to $7.1$s, corresponding to a $24\times$ speedup.
elements.sent-2411-341: On Huh7, CAP matches the best reported $\mathrm{TRA}$ ($0.966$) with the shortest inference time of $3.2$s.
elements.sent-2411-342: As for the extreme instability of the EmbedTrack and EBB methods on Huh7 with a $\mathrm{TRA}$ of $0.187$ and $0.626$, respectively, our analysis suggests that the observed results stem from the method’s excessive reliance on the segmentation (SEG) stage and the usage of refined masks during the training of the segmentation model, while Huh7 contains only SEG golden truth (GT) masks without the more refined SEG silver truth (ST).

elements.sent-2411-343: Overall, as shown in~\cref{charts , the CAP method demonstrates promising performance and constant high efficiency on inference and label usage across all datasets, consistently achieving high $\mathrm{TRA}$ scores and the lowest $\mathrm{IT}$s.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Thus, CAP is a highly effective, efficient, and reliable method for cell tracking in a practical biomedical field.

elements.sent-2411-347: The results demonstrate CAP’s ability to robustly maintain cell identities over extended sequences, even in the presence of densely packed regions, complex motion patterns, and various motion speeds.
elements.sent-2411-348: This consistency highlights CAP’s strength in long-term trajectory preservation and cross-frame association accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-349: CAP successfully identifies division points and accurately tracks the transition from mother cells (M) to daughter cells (D1 and D2), ensuring continuity across frames.
elements.sent-2411-350: The highlighted examples show that CAP not only maintains the mother–daughter relationships but also effectively disambiguates multiple simultaneous divisions within crowded environments.
elements.sent-2411-351: These results underscore the model’s capacity to reconstruct biologically meaningful lineage information, which is critical for downstream analysis in developmental biology and cancer research.
elements.sent-2411-352: We performed extensive experiments to assess the effectiveness of each key component in the CAP framework on both the DeepCell and ISBI CTC datasets: the AEG sampling strategy, the cell joint tracking structure, and the feature stride of the model.
elements.sent-2411-353: To more clearly observe the influence of each key component on individual test sequences, we report the $\mathrm{TRA}$ results for each sequence in the DeepCell datasets.
elements.sent-2411-354: To address the imbalance of cell division events during training, we propose the Adaptive Event-Guided (AEG) sampling strategy (Section~\ref{aeg ), which calibrates the training process by ensuring balanced exposure to complete division sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-355: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:abl_aeg , disabling AEG sampling leads to substantial performance degradation across datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-356: With AEG sampling enabled, the model achieves consistent improvements: for instance, on the HeLa dataset, the best $\mathrm{TRA}$ score rises from $0.898$ to $0.962$, while on U373 it improves from $0.556$ to $0.985$.
elements.sent-2411-357: Similar gains are observed on PC-3 and RAW264.
elements.sent-2411-358: These results confirm that AEG sampling effectively balances event occurrences and significantly enhances the model’s ability to learn from imbalanced data.

elements.sent-2411-359: We evaluate the role of joint tracking by replacing cross-trajectory attention layers with time-only attention while maintaining model size.
elements.sent-2411-360: As reported in Table~\ref{tab:joint , this modification consistently reduces performance, with $\mathrm{TRA}$ drops ranging from $0.015$ to $0.055$ across datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-361: Incorporating both time and joint attention yields notable improvements, particularly on HeLa where the $\mathrm{TRA}$ increases from $0.853$ to $0.962$ in the best case.
elements.sent-2411-362: This demonstrates that the dual-attention design not only strengthens temporal coherence but also captures relational dependencies between trajectories, leading to more robust tracking in complex cellular environments.

elements.sent-2411-363: Table~\ref{tab:stride highlights the effect of feature stride on model accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-364: Reducing the stride from $8$ to $4$ substantially improves performance by providing higher-resolution features.
elements.sent-2411-365: For example, on the HeLa dataset, the best $\mathrm{TRA}$ score increases from $0.884$ to $0.962$, while on U373 the score rises from $0.531$ to $0.985$.
elements.sent-2411-366: Similar trends are observed across 3T3 and RAW264, demonstrating that higher-resolution features are critical for capturing fine-grained cell dynamics and improving tracking accuracy, especially in challenging datasets with small or densely packed cells.
elements.sent-2411-367: Consequently, we adopt $s=4$ as the default stride setting in CAP framework.
elements.sent-2411-368: In this paper, we introduce the one-stage CAP (Cell as Point) framework, which eliminates the need for a separate segmentation or detection stage, which is a significant innovation in cell tracking.
elements.sent-2411-369: This approach substantially improves efficiency by reducing inference time by a factor of $8$ to $32$ and minimizing the need for high-quality data annotation.
elements.sent-2411-370: CAP effectively captures complex cell states and tracks cells in one stage while maintaining high efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-371: The limitation of our study arises from the nature of the datasets used for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-372: These datasets were annotated by different experts, and variations in annotation practices may lead to differences in the location of the cell points that CAP generated.
elements.sent-2411-373: Such inconsistencies can introduce discrepancies in performance across datasets, as the model could be sensitive to variations in label quality and annotation style.
elements.sent-2411-374: In addition, differences in imaging devices and cell densities across datasets may further amplify these effects, contributing to dataset-specific performance variation.
elements.sent-2411-375: Looking forward, a promising direction is to design strategies that mitigate the impact of heterogeneous annotations and dataset-specific biases, thereby reducing sensitivity to labeling differences.
elements.sent-2411-376: Consequently, extending CAP toward a more versatile universal model that can generalize effectively across diverse datasets and imaging conditions deserves future exploration.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
